critique.summary: The article proposes classifying happiness as a psychiatric disorder, termed 'major affective disorder, pleasant type.' It argues that happiness is statistically abnormal, involves a distinct cluster of symptoms, is linked to cognitive abnormalities, and may reflect abnormal central nervous system functioning.
mainPoints: Happiness is statistically abnormal compared to typical mental states.
Happiness consists of a discrete cluster of symptoms similar to psychiatric conditions.
Happiness is associated with cognitive abnormalities.
Happiness may reflect abnormal functioning of the central nervous system.
The common objection that happiness is not negatively valued is dismissed as scientifically irrelevant.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a highly unconventional and controversial hypothesis that happiness should be classified as a psychiatric disorder. This view is not supported by mainstream psychiatric or psychological consensus, which generally considers happiness a positive mental state rather than a disorder. The claim that happiness is statistically abnormal and associated with cognitive abnormalities lacks broad empirical support and contradicts extensive research on subjective well-being. The article is a provocative theoretical proposal rather than an evidence-based conclusion and does not reflect accepted diagnostic criteria or clinical practice.
considerations: Happiness is widely regarded as a positive and desirable emotional state in psychological research and clinical practice.
Current major diagnostic manuals such as the DSM-5 and ICD-11 do not classify happiness or positive affect as disorders.
The article does not address the social, cultural, and ethical implications of pathologizing happiness.
The proposal ignores extensive research on the benefits of happiness for mental and physical health.
The dismissal of the objection that happiness is not negatively valued overlooks the importance of subjective well-being in mental health.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around a provocative paper proposing happiness as a psychiatric disorder, with most commenters interpreting it as a critique of psychiatry's tendency to over-diagnose. Some highlight the paper's deeper message about the limitations and values in psychiatric classification, while others respond with humor or skepticism.

comments.author: eouw0o83hf
comments.text:
I really liked this paper. I think it's less of an outright joke that it's possible to squint your eyes and laugh that happiness could be a disorder, and more of shining a light on the psychopathological system that tends towards over-diagnosis and hyperfixation on those diagnoses.

"If our so-called scientific system were really objective and honest, it would include happiness as a disorder." I think this is the goal the paper is trying to expose, more than just making a joke about mapping a good feeling to a description of a bad feeling. Indeed, I think the last line of the paper gives it away - our current system is very incomplete and needs to be extended:

> Indeed, only a psychopathology that openly declares the relevance of values to classification could persist in excluding happiness from the psychiatric disorders.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced interpretation of the paper's intent, emphasizing its critique of psychiatric classification rather than a literal proposal, aligning well with the article's tone and content.

comments.author: letharion
comments.text: I'm assuming this is some kind of jab at the general propensity of psychiatry to classify most things as disorders, rather than a serious proposal. If anything, I think the problem has gotten worse since this was published. (Then again, maybe happiness has also gotten more rare since 1992?)
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies the paper as a critique of psychiatric over-classification, though it is somewhat speculative about the author's intent.

comments.author: adyashakti
comments.text: it's Catch-22. the world is such a mess that if you're happy, you must be delusional.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a philosophical perspective on happiness in a troubled world, reflecting a common sentiment but less directly connected to the paper's academic critique.

comments.author: AnimalMuppet
comments.text:
<checks calendar> Wait, this isn't April 1st!

Seriously, happiness is a psychiatric disorder? Rare, sure, but a disorder? That's the craziest thing I've heard since... well, since the Iran war, I guess, so not very long. Still, that's nuts. I cannot imagine the world view that it must take to look at happiness that way.
comments.analysis: Expresses incredulity and skepticism about the idea, which is reasonable, though it lacks deeper engagement with the paper's critical intent.

comments.author: techblueberry
comments.text: Ahh 1992.  At the time he probably didn’t know he needed to add a /s or he’d be taken seriously in our delusional future.
comments.analysis: This sarcastic comment implies the paper was misunderstood, but it adds little substantive analysis.

comments.author: boesboes
comments.text: Reminds me of https://thenewinquiry.com/book-of-lamentations/ edit: A review of the DSM as if it where a dystopian novel basically, makes some interesting observations/points
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant external reference that critiques psychiatric classification, though it does not directly analyze the paper itself.

comments.author: pogue
comments.text:
This reminds me of this old gem from The Onion:

FDA Approves Depressant Drug For The Annoyingly Cheerful [video/NSFW/2:06] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jd4tugPM83c
comments.analysis: Uses humor and satire to respond, which is entertaining but not analytically valuable.

comments.author: kusokurae
comments.text:
Reminded of that episode of House where the lady with dormant syphillis had something like this.

I wonder are there any ways I can contract this without breaking marital vows
comments.analysis: Off-topic and unclear comment that adds no meaningful insight to the discussion.

comments.author: dmschulman
comments.analysis: A dismissive one-word comment that does not contribute to the conversation.
